paragraphs.text: audience measurement coalition

section_path: AMC response to the call for evidence on the Digital Fairness Act

paragraphs.text: The Audience Measurement Coalition (AMC) would like to thank the European Commission for the opportunity to contribute to the call for evidence on the forthcoming Digital Fairness Act Proposal. For decades the members of the AMC have ensured that Governments and Ministries across Europe have had access to reliable, neutral and impartial audience measurement data to inform various policies and enforce consumer protection legislation. The most notable uses relate to the protection of minors and vulnerable audiences within the media sphere. JICs and research suppliers regularly share data with ministries across Europe in connection with AVMSD enforcement. Although demand for such data remains, the lack of formal recognition of independent audience measurement in public enforcement has made the independent audience measurement sector vulnerable to the vagaries of digital disruption, changed power structures and new priorities in the digital market. For years the independent audience measurement sector was supported by a wide self-regulatory agreement supporting market transparency in the media sector. In turn, this understanding, organically facilitated public authorities' access to reliable audience measurement. As the power dynamics in the digital media sphere have changed, the market consensus around industry supported audience measurement with trusted third parties has been challenged. Consequently, much needed data for enforcement is increasingly met with opacity in wide areas of the digital media market.

section_path: AMC response to the call for evidence on the Digital Fairness Act

evidence_quotes.quote: reliable independent audience measurement data, including on minors

paragraphs.text: The AMC remains agnostic when it comes to substantial rules that would address or alleviate consumer harms. Nonetheless, we feel it important to point out a structural vulnerability: reliable independent audience measurement data, including on minors, is a fundamental necessity for the assessment of consumer protection policies and their enforcement.

section_path: AMC response to the call for evidence on the Digital Fairness Act

evidence_quotes.quote: report audience measurement numbers through independent industry-agreed channels.

paragraphs.text: We thus believe that any objective in the DFA should be supported by a requirement that actors that operate digital media and advertising, report audience measurement numbers through independent industry-agreed channels.

section_path: AMC response to the call for evidence on the Digital Fairness Act

paragraphs.text: The AMC submitted a response to the Commission's call for evidence on the consumer agenda 2025-2030 in August. We take the liberty of resubmitting this response in connection with the DFA as our observations remain the same in this context. As we speak for the importance of independent audience measurement in informing policy and enforcement, we take the liberty of providing in annex multiple examples on use cases of independent audience measurement by public authorities.

section_path: AMC response to the call for evidence on the Digital Fairness Act

evidence_quotes.quote: data-driven practices that can undermine consumer choice and wellbeing

paragraphs.text: The rapidly evolving digital landscape presents significant challenges for consumer protection, particularly concerning new technologies and data-driven practices that can undermine consumer choice and wellbeing, particularly when it comes to media and advertising consumption. A key difficulty lies in applying existing and future consumer laws to digital environments, as existing transparency mechanisms ensured by independent audience measurement are facing roadblocks. For decades independent audience measurement has provided foundational transparency across the board ensuring both market fairness and public transparency. Identifying where unfair commercial practices occur (which platforms, which media outlets) and whom (categories of consumers) they affect is the first step to any policy-making and enforcement.

evidence_quotes.quote: dark patterns
misleading influencer marketing due to blurred lines between “user generated” and commercial/professional media content

paragraphs.text: In this response, the Audience Measurement Coalition (AMC), addresses the challenges posed by data-driven practices that undermine consumer choice, consumer mental health, unfair commercial practices linked to dark patterns, misleading influencer marketing due to blurred lines between “user generated” and commercial/professional media content, and addictive digital product design. Independent audience measurement is crucial to identify where the problems lie and whom the unfair commercial practices targets.

evidence_quotes.quote: transparent and neutral data regarding content consumption and how the content impacts the consumer.

paragraphs.text: It is clear that to address issues that affect consumer behaviour, especially in relation to content consumption, it is essential to have a benchmark of truth. That means transparent and neutral data regarding content consumption and how the content impacts the consumer.

It has been evident for decades in the context of traditional media, that independent data provided through industry-agreed structures provide the official benchmarks upon which regulators and policy-makers base their policies. However, attention to the availability of reliable data for online media has insufficiently been addressed by EU regulation, with the exception of recent landmark legislative texts such as Digital Markets Act and European Media Freedom Act. Although market-agreed structures such as Joint Industry Committees and Research suppliers measure online consumption, the risks have increased in an environment made up of several non-collaborative digital actors combined with the absence of a specific data protection and privacy framework for audience measurement that has caused blind spots for transparency and independent measurement across the digital media and content market.

evidence_quotes.quote: real risks of potential bias of first party reported data

paragraphs.text: We often see that despite its reach into many aspects of digital media, Regulation itself fails to address the need for reliable audience data in spite of the highly consequential decisions they inform. Reporting obligations often do not address the real risks of potential bias of first party reported data which is by definition self-reporting by stakeholders themselves. There can be little scrutiny as to the accuracy of information provided, nor any mechanism to correct.

Public institutions thus struggle to effectively enforce the protections they intend to enforce, with minors' online behaviour being a commonly reported challenge. This essentially highlights a structural enforcement vulnerability in the digital ecosystem.
It should therefore be an inherent part of the DNA of any new consumer protection legislation that monitoring and enforcement should be based on impartial and reliable audience measurement data.

evidence_quotes.quote: the independence and neutrality of third parties elected by the market to produce audience reports,

paragraphs.text: Historically and currently, the independence and neutrality of third parties elected by the market to produce audience reports, has ensured that players of all sizes can compete fairly on the media market and that public authorities can monitor and enforce their policies based on a benchmark of truth.
**As a foundational transparency mechanism independent measurement helps identify media and advertising consumption patterns and identify consumer protection gaps:**

Understanding how consumers interact with different platforms and content serves as a foundation to assess where consumer choice might be undermined or where existing laws are ambiguous in their application.
**Transparency and fair competition**

evidence_quotes.quote: Independent audience measurement acts as an objective tool for smaller players such as European SMEs, ensuring that market dominance is not a determining factor in the distribution of financial resources on the media market.
This directly counters practices that might undermine consumer choice by favoring dominant players (both advertisers and media players) or enable unfair practices.

By providing an impartial view of audience reach and engagement, it helps clarify market dynamics and thus identify where consumer laws might be failing to ensure that the consumer has a wide range of choices.

paragraphs.text: Independent audience measurement acts as an objective tool for smaller players such as European SMEs, ensuring that market dominance is not a determining factor in the distribution of financial resources on the media market. This directly counters practices that might undermine consumer choice by favoring dominant players (both advertisers and media players) or enable unfair practices. By providing an impartial view of audience reach and engagement, it helps clarify market dynamics and thus identify where consumer laws might be failing to ensure that the consumer has a wide range of choices.

We recognise, for example, that Policy makers are reviewing rules and new proposals around prominence (findability, discoverability) of content. Much focus is on ensuring citizens have access to a plurality of content, and to ensure that dominance is kept in check. The issues are well covered in CERRE's 2023 paper "Towards Coherent Rules On The Prominence Of Media Content On Online Platforms And Digital Devices"³.

The AMC's view is that without a framework of enforced independent audience measurement that allows regulators to see reliable data on the real outcomes of compliance efforts, impact assessments will be inadequate.
**Influencer marketing**
The distinction between "user-generated" and professional media content has become increasingly blurred,

evidence_quotes.quote: receiving sponsorships and advertising revenue.
paid endorsements
advertisements disguised as authentic user content.

paragraphs.text: primarily as both types of content now actively participate in commercial activities, receiving sponsorships and advertising revenue. This can make it challenging for consumers to discern genuine personal opinions or experiences from paid endorsements or professionally produced advertisements disguised as authentic user content.

evidence_quotes.quote: media "influencer" endeavours

paragraphs.text: The delineation between professional and user generated is increasingly outdated as the platform economy has made way for self-employed, micro-enterprise types of media "influencer" endeavours, some even evolving to become multi-million-euro businesses. This is the "creator" economy.

evidence_quotes.quote: separate user-generated content vs. commercial endeavours
The Unfair Commercial Practices Directive does not offer explicit definitions nor criteria that helps identify user-generated content that results from a professional activity.

paragraphs.text: Establishing very clear criteria to separate user-generated content vs. commercial endeavours should be a key first step. The Unfair Commercial Practices Directive does not offer explicit definitions nor criteria that helps identify user-generated content that results from a professional activity.

evidence_quotes.quote: Having a substantial number of subscribers, followers, or regular viewers indicates motivation to go beyond a private circle and gain a following that is of interest to advertisers.

paragraphs.text: Equally, the application of AVMSD often hinges on whether the content is professionally produced and disseminated but does not offer insight into user-generated content categorisations. While EMFA defines a "media service" as a service where the principal purpose is to provide programmes or journalistic productions to the general public$^{4}$, it excludes user-generated content uploaded to an online platform unless it constitutes a professional activity normally provided for consideration, be it of a financial or other nature. EMFA does not provide clear benchmarks to identify user-generated content that would constitute a professional activity. It is however clear from the definition of a "media service", that the size and reach of audiences is a key factor in its definition. It thus also gives a strong indication as to the commercial motivations of a content creator. Having a substantial number of subscribers, followers, or regular viewers indicates motivation to go beyond a private circle and gain a following that is of interest to advertisers.

evidence_quotes.quote: To be able to identify content creators that engage in influencer marketing, impartial assessment of the size and reach of their audiences should be a cornerstone of any new consumer regulation.

paragraphs.text: To protect the consumer, authorities must be able to identify the actors that fall under the scope of consumer law underpinned by evidence-based analysis of consumer behaviour rather than superficial categorisations such as "content creation", "user generated" vs "media service". Currently, influencers' "user-generated" content is opaque and reported privately through non-public proprietary audience measurement systems. To be able to identify content creators that engage in influencer marketing, impartial assessment of the size and reach of their audiences should be a cornerstone of any new consumer regulation.

evidence_quotes.quote: deceptive influencer practices.

paragraphs.text: In this space, independent audience measurement can significantly enhance transparency by providing neutral, evidence-based data on content consumption patterns. By objectively identifying who is watching what, and how content is affecting them, exposing instances where commercial interests are obscured, and thus supporting public authorities with the reliable data needed to monitor and enforce consumer protection policies against deceptive influencer practices.

evidence_quotes.quote: usage data on this cohort is extremely protected.

paragraphs.text: Of importance in this area is the need to recognise that a high degree of consumption is carried out by minors. It is observed that platforms willingness or ability to provide usage data on this cohort is extremely protected. This is counter to the culture, obligations and principles adhered to by traditional media under AVMSD.
$^{4}$ Regulation (EU) 2024/1083, Article 2 (1)

evidence_quotes.quote: Independent audience measurement helps identify how a child's behaviour changes and shifts from one platform to another.
This data is crucial for governments and industries to respond to emerging threats and identify access to harmful online content by minors.

paragraphs.text: Independent audience measurement helps identify how a child's behaviour changes and shifts from one platform to another. This data is crucial for governments and industries to respond to emerging threats and identify access to harmful online content by minors.

evidence_quotes.quote: gaming platforms and other gaming services
the online gaming space is largely opaque
video game measurement at the request of advertisers

paragraphs.text: It is undeniable that there is substantial financial investment in gaming platforms and other gaming services and an increasing reliance on gaming as an advertising channel. The actors operate within their own walled garden and the data reported by these services are proprietary and self-reported. Although independent audience measurement suppliers provide video game measurement at the request of advertisers and non-for profit organisations the online gaming space is largely opaque. Corrective technologies and advanced market research techniques are required to make up for the blind spots and lack of information from the online gaming sector. This issue is to be taken particularly seriously in light of the fact that these forums have become hubs for minors' media consumption. For numerous younger demographics, gaming has evolved beyond simple recreation into a multifaceted environment for social interaction, learning and media consumption. Gaming platforms and other types of structures have integrated film, television, and literature into their services. It is thus important to note that media consumption now flows through the gaming platforms, not only through social media or other on demand services.

evidence_quotes.quote: that can be highly addictive and manipulative.
loot boxes

paragraphs.text: Many online games, particularly those with "free-to-play" models, employ design elements and monetization strategies (e.g., loot boxes, in-game purchases, battle passes) that can be highly addictive and manipulative.

evidence_quotes.quote: These designs can exploit psychological vulnerabilities
problematic gaming behavior

paragraphs.text: These designs can exploit psychological vulnerabilities, leading to excessive spending, problematic gaming behavior, and even gambling-like harms, especially among children and vulnerable individuals.

evidence_quotes.quote: "pay-to-win" schemes
in-game harassment
influencer marketing that blurs advertising with genuine gameplay

paragraphs.text: Children, in particular, are exposed to risks such as age-inappropriate content, in-game harassment, and misleading marketing tactics (e.g., "pay-to-win" schemes, influencer marketing that blurs advertising with genuine gameplay). As with other digital environments, applying traditional consumer protection laws to the nuances of online gaming can be challenging. The rapid evolution of game mechanics and monetization models often outpaces regulatory frameworks, creating gaps in enforcement.

evidence_quotes.quote: the gaming market often involves influencer marketing
influencer marketing

paragraphs.text: Additionally, the gaming market often involves influencer marketing that blurs advertising with genuine gameplay. Independent audience measurement can help determine the actual reach and engagement of content creators within gaming, ensuring transparency over commercial motivations.

evidence_quotes.quote: Independent audience measurement should be a requirement on gaming platforms for basic transparency and reporting purposes.
They should prove to authorities that they comply with the same consumer protective measures when it comes to online media (such as advertising restrictions).
In addition, independent audience measurement is crucial to identify how children's and other users' behaviors change

paragraphs.text: Independent audience measurement should be a requirement on gaming platforms for basic transparency and reporting purposes. They should prove to authorities that they comply with the same consumer protective measures when it comes to online media (such as advertising restrictions). In addition, independent audience measurement is crucial to identify how children's and other users' behaviors change

evidence_quotes.quote: addictive design elements
loot boxes

paragraphs.text: and shift across different online gaming platforms so as to is essential identify patterns related to addictive design elements and manipulative monetization strategies (e.g., loot boxes, in-game purchases, battle passes) that can lead to excessive spending or problematic gaming behavior.
Human attention is emerging as a vulnerable resource in the digital environment, subject to commercial exploitation with hitherto lacking in sufficient safeguards.

evidence_quotes.quote: endless scrolls, constant notifications, autoplay videos
hyper-personalised content-generation

paragraphs.text: Excessive “attention harvesting” – through endless scrolls, constant notifications, autoplay videos, and other tactics – is increasingly recognised as harmful to individual autonomy and public health. The roll out of AI-based agents with hyper-personalised content-generation risks accelerating the problem.

evidence_quotes.quote: banning certain manipulative practices

paragraphs.text: Today no EU law explicitly protects a person’s “attentional integrity” as a right in itself. Existing regulations like the Digital Services Act (DSA), Digital Markets Act (DMA), and proposed AI Act address pieces of the problem (e.g. banning certain manipulative practices or mandating data transparency), but they do so in a fragmented way.

evidence_quotes.quote: pervasive design techniques that maximise time-on-screen

paragraphs.text: There remains a regulatory gap: pervasive design techniques that maximise time-on-screen – even at the cost of user well-being – fall between the cracks of current law.

evidence_quotes.quote: Without clearer boundaries, platforms will continue to push the limits of attentional extraction.

paragraphs.text: Without clearer boundaries, platforms will continue to push the limits of attentional extraction. This not only undermines user welfare, but also weakens media pluralism and fair competition, as gatekeepers hold proprietary data on user attention that outsiders cannot verify.
In short, European digital policy must evolve to treat attention with the same seriousness as data protection or content moderation and demand that platforms are independently measured and audited.

evidence_quotes.quote: Inconsistent interpretations by national Data Protection Authorities are, in many instances, forcing independent audience measurement providers to rely on consent, creating data blind spots that hinder transparency in digital media.

paragraphs.text: Inconsistent interpretations by national Data Protection Authorities are, in many instances, forcing independent audience measurement providers to rely on consent, creating data blind spots that hinder transparency in digital media.

section_path: Independent audience measurement is the ally of the consumer: a proper privacy and data protection framework

paragraphs.text: This is highly problematic for the effective public interest role of independent third-party measurement, which provides anonymized, impartial data crucial for a functioning digital economy and public transparency. To address this, we advocate for explicit recognition that independent audience measurement, with proper definitions and safeguards, is compatible with the GDPR's public interest legal basis (Article 6(1)(e) GDPR) or the fulfillment of a legal obligation (Article 6.1 c) GDPR in the fulfilment of the principles in EMFA article 24. Equally, the ePrivacy Directive should allow flexibility for data collection for this purpose, given proper user information.

section_path: Independent audience measurement is the ally of the consumer: a proper privacy and data protection framework

evidence_quotes.quote: Independent audience measurement, delivered by neutral JICs and research suppliers provides critical, evidence-based data that can inform regulatory responses to the challenges of consumer protection in the digital age.

paragraphs.text: Independent audience measurement, delivered by neutral JICs and research suppliers provides critical, evidence-based data that can inform regulatory responses to the challenges of consumer protection in the digital age.

evidence_quotes.quote: monitoring the consumption patterns of vulnerable groups such as children.

paragraphs.text: By ensuring market transparency, aiding in the detection of fraudulent activities, and crucially, monitoring the consumption patterns of vulnerable groups such as children. Its impartial nature and adherence to high market research standards make it an indispensable tool for public authorities and stakeholders committed to safeguarding consumers in an increasingly complex digital environment.

Future regulation aiming to protect consumers must establish clear benchmarks based on evidence-based observation of real consumer behaviour, which includes reliance on independent audience measurement.

evidence_quotes.quote: must be independently monitored.

paragraphs.text: Digital actors that engage in media and advertising must be independently monitored. Public authorities should not be forced to rely on assumptions, public relations claims or self-reporting of audience numbers. Limiting audience transparency to traditional media services does not align with the reality of current consumption patterns, particularly those of minors.

Independent audience measurement providers must be able to fulfill their mission and thus supported by EU data protection and privacy law. Independent organisations that provide official market-agreed audience measurement to public authorities must be covered by the public interest legal basis in the GDPR (article 6.1 (e) of Regulation (EU) 2016/679).

The Audience Measurement Coalition (AMC) is an association that serves as the voice of the independent audience measurement sector, which includes research suppliers and Joint Industry Committees (JICs) including AGF (Germany), AGMA (Germany), ARMA (Romania), CAEM (Portugal), CIM (Belgium), Danske Medier Research (Denmark), Finnpanel (Finland), Mediapulse (Switzerland), Médiamétrie (France), MMS (Sweden), Media Metrics Finland Oy (Finland), NMO (Netherlands), Norwegian MOC, TAM Ireland, WEMF (Switzerland), Comscore, GfK, Ipsos, Nielsen, Kantar and Streamhub. For decades the members of the AMC have served European markets to ensure that both traditional and digital publishers' and broadcasters' number of active users (audiences) are measured correctly. Independent audience measurement serves as the backbone of Europe's media industry, providing invaluable insights into consumption patterns for media companies. Additionally, it supports public service media in fulfilling their mandates and assists advertisers and public authorities in making informed decisions.
https://audiencemeasurementcoalition.eu/
E-mail: info@audiencemeasurementcoalition.eu

Industry agreed audience measurement data plays a crucial role for governments, regulatory bodies, and public authorities in fulfilling their missions. Across Europe, data provided by research companies and Joint Industry Committees (JICs) is leveraged in numerous ways. Below is a list of examples illustrating how this data supports public decision-making.

section_path: ANNEX- Industry Agreed Audience Measurement: how it is used in the public interest

paragraphs.text: As media consumption increasingly shifts to digital platforms, gathering end-user data has become essential to produce accurate audience measurement reports that adhere to the highest market research standards. Understanding end-user activity is crucial for generating these insights, as demonstrated by the use cases below.

section_path: ANNEX- Industry Agreed Audience Measurement: how it is used in the public interest

paragraphs.text: Striking the right balance when designing and conducting audience measurement is key: it influences a wide range of decisions, including those related to public interest and policy where Governments, member states, and markets rely on validated industry-agreed data to guide their decision-making.

section_path: ANNEX- Industry Agreed Audience Measurement: how it is used in the public interest

paragraphs.text: | Country | Use cases | | --- | --- | | Belgium | CIM audience measurement data is primarily used by Belgium's Community Governments and their Media Ministries to evaluate media policies, under the jurisdiction of Flanders, the Fédération Wallonie-Bruxelles, and the German Speaking Community. Public service broadcasters, who are members of CIM, use this data to demonstrate societal impact and compliance with KPIs outlined in charter agreements with governments. The annual 2023 report can be found hereCIM audience data for movies and series is reported annually to and by the Commissions that co-finance (or subsidise) audiovisual productions. Some | | | companies and other public sector organisations are not available. This is because the public sector is not always aware of the public sector's policies and policies. The CIM audience measurement data is also primarily used by Belgium's Community Governments and their Media Ministries to evaluate media policies, under the jurisdiction of Flanders, the Fédération Wallonie-Bruxelles, and the German Speaking Community. Public service broadcasters, who are members of CIM, use this data to demonstrate societal impact and compliance with KPIs outlined in charter agreements with governments. The annual 2023 report can be found hereCIM audience data for movies and series is reported annually to and by the Commissions that co-finance (or subsidise) audiovisual productions. Some |

paragraphs.text: | | Commissions are overseen by the Culture departments of the Communities, and the economic support funds are overseen by the Regions (Flanders, Brussels and Wallonia). For further reading see here.

section_path: ANNEX- Industry Agreed Audience Measurement: how it is used in the public interest

paragraphs.text: CIM data is used by media Regulators to determine how high a fine should be as higher fines are given to large TV or radio stations when breaking sponsorship or gambling regulations by the Flemish Regulator (“VRM”). VRM also publishes a yearly report on media concentration and uses CIM data to calculate concentration indexes. You can read the 2023 report here.

section_path: ANNEX- Industry Agreed Audience Measurement: how it is used in the public interest

paragraphs.text: Equally, the Belgian Competition Authority BMA/ABC uses CIM data for evaluations. This can be seen in the RTL JV by Rossel case and DPG Media case. For further reading consult pages 50, 85, 153, 179 here.

section_path: ANNEX- Industry Agreed Audience Measurement: how it is used in the public interest

paragraphs.text: It is to be noted that the CrossMedia Measurement initiative (XMM) is ongoing and is subsidised by the Flemish Government. CIM is the project manager on XMM and its data and panel (XMP) will be integrated in CIM measurements to guarantee the continuity of the project. XMC must deliver to the VRM and also to a consortium of academic partners for other research initiatives aiming to strengthen the local media ecosystem. To read about the initiative, see here | | --- | --- | | Croatia | The public broadcaster (HRT) is a regular user of audience measurement data from the TV JIC UTMG, utilising it for planning purposes in the same way as commercial TV stations.

section_path: ANNEX- Industry Agreed Audience Measurement: how it is used in the public interest

evidence_quotes.quote: compliance with advertising regulations
production quotas

paragraphs.text: The Agency for Electronic Media (AEM), a regulatory body under the Croatian Ministry of Culture, also relies on this data. They use monthly monitoring reports to assess broadcasters' compliance with advertising regulations and production quotas.

section_path: ANNEX- Industry Agreed Audience Measurement: how it is used in the public interest

paragraphs.text: State-owned companies involved in advertising, such as Hrvatska Lutrija (the Croatian Lottery), HEP (the national electrical utility), the Health Insurance Fund (HZZO), and various ministry departments for project-based campaigns funded by the EU, use the data indirectly. These companies select agencies through public tenders, with the requirement that the chosen agency must be a regular subscriber to audience measurement data and be experienced in media (TV) planning. | | Czech Republic | In the Czech Republic, market-agreed data is regularly utilised by the Council for Radio and Television Broadcasting (RRTV). Similarly to Poland, RRTV monitors broadcasters' compliance with sector-specific regulations, such as advertising share, permissible time slots for alcohol ads, and the enforcement of measures to protect minors. They rely on this data daily to ensure compliance.

section_path: ANNEX- Industry Agreed Audience Measurement: how it is used in the public interest

paragraphs.text: Additionally, the Office for Oversight of Financing of Political Parties and Movements uses this data to track political parties' advertisement spending during election campaigns. |

section_path: ANNEX- Industry Agreed Audience Measurement: how it is used in the public interest

paragraphs.text: | Denmark | The Danish Ministry of Culture has an office responsible for media oversight, subscribing to market-agreed audience measurement data, which it uses on an ad hoc basis. This data informs their annual reports on the Danish media landscape, such as the example found here, and is also used for broadcast permissions, tenders, and to follow up on "public service reports" from broadcasters who have won tenders.

section_path: ANNEX- Industry Agreed Audience Measurement: how it is used in the public interest

evidence_quotes.quote: rely on audience data in their work,

paragraphs.text: Several committees and boards under the ministry also rely on audience data in their work, including the Radio- og tv-nævnet (Danish Authority for Radio and TV), Medienævnet (Danish Media Regulatory Authority), Bladpuljens | | --- | --- | | | Fordelingsudvalg (agency for press subsidies), and the Medierådet for Børn og Unge (Council for Children and Adolescents).

section_path: ANNEX- Industry Agreed Audience Measurement: how it is used in the public interest

paragraphs.text: The Danish Parliament (Folketinget) frequently references audience data when responding to “§20 questions” (open questions from MPs to the government) on media-related topics. Furthermore, Danmarks Statistik (the Danish Institute for Statistics) incorporates audience measurement data into its publicly available statistics.

section_path: ANNEX- Industry Agreed Audience Measurement: how it is used in the public interest

paragraphs.text: Both DR (the public service broadcaster) and TV2 (a state-owned commercial channel) are members of the Media Owners Committee (MOC) and Joint Industry Committees (JICs), relying heavily on audience data. The Ministry of Economy (Erhvervsministeriet), which oversees technical infrastructure, also uses audience measurement data to assess the public’s usage of different media technologies such as broadcasting, streaming, FM, and DAB. | | --- | --- | | Finland | The Ministry of Transportation and Communications, along with the Finnish Transportation and Communications Agency (Traficom), have been subscribed to Finpannel’s Establishment Survey data for many years. This data provides insights into infrastructure, equipment, and basic viewing behaviour in Finnish private households. Additionally, they regularly utilise publicly available viewing data and analysis in their work.

section_path: ANNEX- Industry Agreed Audience Measurement: how it is used in the public interest

paragraphs.text: The Finnish public broadcasting company Yleisradio (Yle) is a major subscriber to audience measurement data from Finnpanel. Yle uses this data for various purposes, including demonstrating to public officials that it reaches a significant percentage of the Finnish population on a daily, weekly, and monthly basis. |

section_path: ANNEX- Industry Agreed Audience Measurement: how it is used in the public interest

paragraphs.text: | France | In France, public policy is increasingly shaped by audience data. Public authorities often consider the number of unique users of services as crucial information for policy decisions. For example, the French Parliament evaluates the performance of public television based on unique users and reach. However, as media consumption shifts to digital platforms, the requirement for user consent limits the ability to measure certain parts of the population. As a result, the objectives set by Parliament cannot be fully assessed, even though reports are submitted annually.Similarly, the French media regulator ARCOM oversees subsidies for content creation, determining funding based on revenue thresholds and the number of unique users. This applies not only to TV channels but also to large international SVOD platforms. Yet, the challenge of accurately measuring digital consumption persists due to consent requirements, making it difficult to obtain and aggregate comprehensive audience metrics.While public policy increasingly relies on this data, the difficulties in capturing a full digital audience hinder proper evaluation.An example of a report from the French Government using Médiamétrie data can be found here | | --- | --- | | Germany | AGF data is a key resource for measuring TV audience reach and viewer behaviour in Germany. It is widely utilised by public broadcasters, such as ARD and ZDF, regulatory bodies like the Landesmedienanstalten, and various state institutions for several essential purposes:Media Regulation: Regulatory bodies like the KEK use AGF data to monitor media concentration and ensure pluralism, particularly in cases involving mergers between broadcasters. | | | Statistical Publications: The "Statistisches Bundesamt" (Federal Statistical Office) incorporates AGF data into its official publications as can be seen herePublic Health and Nutrition: The Ministry of Health and Nutrition uses AGF data to analyse national media consumption patterns. Usage of such statistics can be see on the Government's page on the draft law on food advertising aimed at children herePublic Broadcasting: Public broadcasters rely on AGF data to assess viewership, plan programming, and justify their public funding.Legal Compliance: AGF data supports the enforcement of media laws that depend on audience figures, helping maintain a diverse and balanced media environment.AGF's daily audience measurement data is crucial not only for public broadcasters but also for many other key media players. This data helps ensure that media power remains fairly distributed and that broadcasters | | --- | --- |

section_path: ANNEX- Industry Agreed Audience Measurement: how it is used in the public interest

paragraphs.text: | | continue to serve the public interest. | | --- | --- | | Italy | In Italy, Joint Industry Committees (JICs) operate under the supervision and guidance of the national regulatory authority AGCOM, with specific obligations regarding what they measure and how they measure audiences. Auditel, the JIC responsible for television, presents an annual report to Parliament and other public officials, providing insights into the state of media, technological trends, consumer habits, and developments in the media industry. For reference, see here.

section_path: ANNEX- Industry Agreed Audience Measurement: how it is used in the public interest

paragraphs.text: Additionally, Auditel data is used by the Ministry for Economic Development to establish criteria for publishers to access the Fund for Pluralism and Innovation (Fondo per il pluralismo e l'innovazione dell'informazione). In the near future, this data will also be critical in evaluating whether publishing companies meet the requirements for funding aimed at digitalization and technological innovation.

section_path: ANNEX- Industry Agreed Audience Measurement: how it is used in the public interest

paragraphs.text: Italian authorities have further mandated that online streaming platforms acquiring Serie A football rights must be measured by a Joint Industry Committee, as seen in the DAZN case for further reading. | | Ireland | In Ireland, JIC Audience Measurement data is frequently utilised by the Media Regulator, Coimisiún na Meán. This regulatory body succeeded the Broadcasting Authority of Ireland (BAI), which was dissolved on March 15, 2023.

section_path: ANNEX- Industry Agreed Audience Measurement: how it is used in the public interest

paragraphs.text: Coimisiún na Meán inherited the regulatory functions of the BAI and has expanded its responsibilities to include establishing a regulatory framework for online safety, updating regulations for television broadcasting and audiovisual on-demand services, and transposing the revised Audiovisual Media Services Directive into Irish law.

section_path: ANNEX- Industry Agreed Audience Measurement: how it is used in the public interest

evidence_quotes.quote: based on audience profiles.

paragraphs.text: With full access to JIC audience measurement data, Coimisiún na Meán uses this information regularly to monitor compliance with the Broadcasting Act and specific codes and standards, such as the Children's Commercial Communications Code. This code includes a definition of 'Children's programmes' based on audience profiles. The data is also employed to assess the placement of ads, particularly those related to High in Fat, Salt, and Sugar (HFSS) products, by evaluating audience profiles. |

section_path: ANNEX- Industry Agreed Audience Measurement: how it is used in the public interest

evidence_quotes.quote: NPO extensively utilises NMO data to inform the programming of content that serves the diverse Dutch population.

paragraphs.text: | **Netherlands** | The Dutch public broadcasting organisation, NPO, is a member of the Dutch Joint Industry Committee, NMO. NPO extensively utilises NMO data to inform the programming of content that serves the diverse Dutch population.

section_path: ANNEX- Industry Agreed Audience Measurement: how it is used in the public interest

paragraphs.text: NMO provides audience data to STER, the government-owned advertising sales organisation responsible for public audio and video channels. This data is crucial for allocating advertising revenue from national advertisers across the 13 public regional channels (both audio and video).

section_path: ANNEX- Industry Agreed Audience Measurement: how it is used in the public interest

paragraphs.text: The Media Regulator, Commissariaat voor de Media (CvdM), relies heavily on NMO data for its various responsibilities. This data is used to audit content delivery on public channels, ensuring that public service obligations are met in accordance with Dutch law.

section_path: ANNEX- Industry Agreed Audience Measurement: how it is used in the public interest

paragraphs.text: Additionally, the Netherlands Authority for Consumers and Markets (ACM) uses NMO audience measurement data to assess and substantiate its opinions on mergers and acquisitions within the media sector, ensuring fair competition and protecting consumer interests. | | --- | --- | | **Poland** | Audience measurement data is a vital tool for the National Broadcasting Council (Krajowa Rada Radiofonii i Telewizji/ KRRiT) to oversee broadcasters' adherence to regulatory standards. This includes monitoring compliance with rules related to advertising share, time slots for alcohol ads, and the implementation of measures to protect children.

section_path: ANNEX- Industry Agreed Audience Measurement: how it is used in the public interest

paragraphs.text: As subscribers to the market-agreed audience measurement data, the National Broadcasting Council utilises this information daily. The data supports their regulatory functions by providing detailed insights into broadcasting practices and ensuring that broadcasters follow established guidelines and standards.

section_path: ANNEX- Industry Agreed Audience Measurement: how it is used in the public interest

paragraphs.text: In addition to enforcing compliance, the National Broadcasting Council uses audience measurement data to assess the effectiveness of regulatory policies and make informed decisions about future regulations. This ongoing use of data helps maintain a balanced and fair broadcasting environment, contributing to the protection of public interests and the promotion of responsible media practices. |

section_path: ANNEX- Industry Agreed Audience Measurement: how it is used in the public interest

paragraphs.text: | Romania | Under Romanian audiovisual law, the National Council of Audiovisual (CNA), which regulates the Romanian audiovisual market, has access to television and radio audience measurement data as required by law.

section_path: ANNEX- Industry Agreed Audience Measurement: how it is used in the public interest

evidence_quotes.quote: “must-carry” principle outlined in both European and Romanian legislation.

paragraphs.text: The Council uses this data to carry out its regulatory functions, as defined by the legislation. For example, the data helps the CNA determine which TV channels must be included by cable carriers and, starting this year, by online video platforms, in accordance with the “must-carry” principle outlined in both European and Romanian legislation.

section_path: ANNEX- Industry Agreed Audience Measurement: how it is used in the public interest

paragraphs.text: Occasionally, other state or public authorities also utilise Romanian television audience measurement (TAM) data for their campaigns on TV. In such instances, the data can be requested directly from ARMA (the Romanian Video JIC) by the interested public authority or through a media agency handling the specific campaign.

section_path: ANNEX- Industry Agreed Audience Measurement: how it is used in the public interest

paragraphs.text: Additionally, the Romanian public broadcasting organisation, TVR, participates in ARMA and is an observer on the Board. | | --- | --- | | Slovenia | AKOS (the Electronic Media Agency) employs market-agreed audience measurement data to ensure that TV stations comply with media regulations. This data allows AKOS to monitor adherence to media laws, including content quotas, advertising rules, and other regulatory requirements. By analysing audience metrics, AKOS can effectively oversee broadcasting practices and enforce compliance with national media standards.

section_path: ANNEX- Industry Agreed Audience Measurement: how it is used in the public interest

evidence_quotes.quote: utilises audience measurement data to assess viewership patterns

paragraphs.text: In addition, the UVZI (Government Office of Slovenia for Information) utilises audience measurement data to assess viewership patterns, particularly for news broadcasts. This data helps UVZI evaluate the reach and impact of news content and track the performance of government representatives. By examining how many people watch specific news segments or programs, UVZI can gauge the effectiveness of public communication and make informed decisions regarding media strategies and government outreach.

section_path: ANNEX- Industry Agreed Audience Measurement: how it is used in the public interest

paragraphs.text: Overall, both AKOS and UVZI leverage audience measurement data to fulfil their respective roles in media regulation and public information assessment, ensuring that media operations align with legal requirements and serve public interests effectively. | | --- | --- |

section_path: ANNEX- Industry Agreed Audience Measurement: how it is used in the public interest

paragraphs.text: | **Sweden** | SVT (Sweden’s Public Broadcaster) is both an owner and a member of MMS (MedieMetrics Sweden), while UR (Swedish Educational Broadcasting Company) is also a member. Both SVT and UR have daily access to audience measurement data, which they use to inform their programming and ensure they meet audience needs.

section_path: ANNEX- Industry Agreed Audience Measurement: how it is used in the public interest

paragraphs.text: Additionally, Post- och Telestyrelsen (the Swedish Post and Telecom Authority) and Mediamyndigheten (Sweden’s Media Regulator) utilise audience measurement data on an ad hoc basis. These organisations use the data to support various regulatory and oversight functions, including monitoring compliance and assessing media market dynamics. | | --- | --- | | **Switzerland** | In Switzerland, Mediapulse operates under the supervision of the Federal Office of Communications (OFCOM). In return for this oversight, Mediapulse receives federal contributions from reception fees for its radio and TV measurement systems.

section_path: ANNEX- Industry Agreed Audience Measurement: how it is used in the public interest

items.topic: Data Collection And Provision To Authorities

evidence_quotes.quote: It collects and provides data on both traditional and new media reception options, ensuring that this information is available to the relevant authorities.

paragraphs.text: Mediapulse plays a crucial role in meeting a key requirement of the Federal Radio and Television Act (RTVA). It collects and provides data on both traditional and new media reception options, ensuring that this information is available to the relevant authorities.

section_path: ANNEX- Industry Agreed Audience Measurement: how it is used in the public interest

evidence_quotes.quote: allocation of national spectrum to broadcast license holders, it needs to have accurate information available on the audiences to channels and stations that license fee holders run.

Note that not all license fee holders subscribe to audience measurement and the associated measurement by the standard audience currencies, but the majority do and this allows more effective oversight by the regulator.

paragraphs.text: OFCOM uses the data provided by Mediapulse to monitor and analyse the development of TV, radio, and online media within the Swiss media landscape. This data is essential for understanding media consumption trends and supporting regulatory and policy decisions. | | **United Kingdom** | Allocation of TV and radio licenses – because Ofcom is in charge of allocating national spectrum to broadcast license holders, it needs to have accurate information available on the audiences to channels and stations that license fee holders run. Note that not all license fee holders subscribe to audience measurement and the associated measurement by the standard audience currencies, but the majority do and this allows more effective oversight by the regulator.

section_path: ANNEX- Industry Agreed Audience Measurement: how it is used in the public interest

paragraphs.text: Competition and media pluralism – audience measurement is used to assess issues relating to news media plurality, particularly when there is a public interest test being carried out as part of a competition assessment or making a broadcast code assessment

section_path: ANNEX- Industry Agreed Audience Measurement: how it is used in the public interest

paragraphs.text: Regulation of public service media including BBC – Ofcom uses TV and radio audience data to uphold the Broadcasting Code and the Communications Act. These contain responsibilities on the content broadcast to viewers and listeners and also bestows a responsibility on Ofcom to stay up to date and report on latest developments in audience viewing trends in the public interest. Whenever a consultation Is launched on existing regulation or proposed changes, audience data is used as an evidence background to these. |

section_path: ANNEX- Industry Agreed Audience Measurement: how it is used in the public interest

paragraphs.text: | | Code on the Scheduling of TV Advertising – auditing of the extent to which COSTA rules are observed by broadcasters are what audience impacts are necessary to limit the scale of advertising on TV channels. This also has implications to other forms of advertising restrictions for example those placed on certain advertisements within programming aimed at or viewed by large numbers of children. | | --- | --- |

section_path: ANNEX- Industry Agreed Audience Measurement: how it is used in the public interest
